1: uzekhor et borekha bimei bechurotekha: 'Remember your Creator in the days of your youth.' Borekha (your Creator) — plural of majesty from bara (to create). The command to zekor (remember) is the covenant call that runs through Deuteronomy — remembering is not merely cognitive recall but the active orientation of life toward what one knows. The bimei bechurotekha (in the days of your prime/youth) connects directly to Ecclesiastes 11:9's commendation of youth, adding the frame: youth is precisely the season for this orientation, before the deterioration described in the extended allegory that follows. Ad asher lo yavo'u yemei hara'ah: 'before the days of trouble come' — the aging and dying that chapter 12 elaborates.
2: ad asher lo techeshah hashemesh veha'or: 'Before the sun and the light grow dark.' The extended allegory of aging begins (Ecclesiastes 12:2-7), using cosmic and architectural imagery for the body's decline. The darkening of sun, light, moon, and stars has been interpreted as either a description of the old person's failing senses or as cosmic dissolution imagery. Veshabu he'avim achar haggeshem: 'and the clouds return after the rain' — unlike in summer when rain brings clearing, in old age the clouds return repeatedly, suggesting the relentless succession of ailments in old age where one illness follows another without full recovery in between.
3: beyom sheyir'azu shomrei habayit: 'In the day when the keepers of the house tremble.' The allegorical interpretation has a long tradition: shomrei habayit (keepers of the house) = arms/hands that tremble with age. Ve'hit'avtu anshei hachayil: 'and the men of valor become bent' = the legs that once bore the body's strength now bow. Uvatellu hatochnot ki me'etu: 'and the grinders cease because they are few' = the teeth. Vechashechu haro'ot ba'arubbot: 'and those who look through the windows grow dim' = the eyes. The body-house allegory transforms the physical decline of aging into a scene of architectural decay — simultaneously more dignified and more poignant than clinical description.
4: vesuggeru delatayim bashuk: 'When the street doors are shut' — possibly the mouth closed from lack of teeth (continuing the body-allegory), or the social withdrawal of old age. Bishfal qol hatachanah: 'when the sound of grinding is low' — the grinding mills (the teeth) silent. Veyaqum leqol hatzippur: 'when one rises at the sound of a bird' — the light sleep of the elderly who wake at any sound. Veyishshachu kol benot hashir: 'and all the daughters of song are brought low' — the voice and hearing fail; the singing and hearing of music that characterized the earlier seasons of life (Ecclesiastes 11:9: the feast and joy) are diminished.
5: gam migavoha yira'u: 'And they will be afraid of heights.' Vechatchtim baderekh: 'and dread is on the path' — the confined world of the aged person who fears heights and dread every journey. Veyafetz hashaqed: 'when the almond tree blossoms' — the white blossoms on the bare branches of the almond tree in late winter, the first tree to blossom, are the image for white hair on an old head. Veyistabbal hechagav: 'when the locust drags itself along' — the labored movement of the aged. Vetafer ha'aviyyonah: 'and the caperberry fails' — possibly an aphrodisiac plant whose failure signals the end of sexual vitality. Ki holekh ha'adam el beit olamo: 'for man goes to his eternal home' — beit olamo (house of his eternity/the grave) — the destination toward which all the failing capacities converge.
6: ad asher lo yeratteq chevvel hakkesef: 'Before the silver cord is severed.' Chevvel hakesef (cord of silver) — the silver cord that breaks; in the body-allegory, possibly the spinal cord or the breath/life-thread. Vetaruts gullat hazahav: 'and the golden bowl is shattered' — the bowl of gold that cracks; the brain, or the lamp that illuminates. Veyishaver kad al hamayyin: 'and the pitcher is broken at the spring' — the lungs or heart at the spring of breath. Venarots hagalal el habor: 'and the wheel breaks at the cistern' — the pulley at the water-well, the heart or the circulatory system. The four broken-things images form a unified picture of the body's cessation — the cord, the bowl, the pitcher, the wheel — each representing a vital system that finally fails.
7: veyashov ha'afar al ha'aretz kashehayyah: 'The dust returns to the earth as it was.' The direct citation of Genesis 3:19: ki afar atah ve'el afar tashuv (for you are dust and to dust you shall return). Qohelet's entire preceding allegory has been elaborating this single verse from Genesis. Veharuach tashuv el ha'elohim asher netanah: 'and the spirit returns to God who gave it.' The body returns to its material origin; the spirit (ruach) returns to its divine source. This is not a detailed theology of the afterlife but an acknowledgment that the life-breath is God's gift from beginning to end, returned to the Giver at death. The netanah (who gave it) frames the entire life between gift and return.
8: havel havalim amar haqohelet hakol havel: 'Vapor of vapors, says the Teacher — all is vapor.' The book closes (or near-closes) with the opening refrain of Ecclesiastes 1:2 — the inclusio that frames the entire work. Between the opening and closing havel havalim, Qohelet has observed kings and fools, wisdom and madness, labor and rest, feasts and funerals, youth and old age, the living and the dead. The final havel is not nihilistic despair but the considered conclusion of comprehensive observation: the transience of all things is the consistent finding of careful attention to reality.
9: veyoter shehayyah qohelet chakham: 'Beyond this, the Teacher was wise.' The epilogue (Ecclesiastes 12:9-14) shifts from first-person discourse to a third-person description of Qohelet's work. Od limmad da'at et ha'am: 'he also taught knowledge to the people.' Ve'izen vichiqer tiqen meshalim harbeh: 'he listened, investigated, and arranged many proverbs.' Izen (listened, weighed carefully) — from ozen (ear); the process of careful reception. Chiqer (searched, investigated) — from chaqar, to probe. Tiqen (arranged, set in order) — the final editorial work of giving structure to what was received and investigated. The three-stage wisdom process: receptive listening, active investigation, careful arrangement.
10: biqqesh qohelet limtzo divrei cheifetz: 'The Teacher sought to find pleasing words.' Divrei cheifetz (words of delight/pleasure) — not superficially entertaining but deeply satisfying words. Vekatuv yosher divrei emet: 'and written uprightly — words of truth.' Yosher (uprightness, honesty) and emet (truth, reliability) — the two standards that governed the Teacher's writing. The double commitment — to pleasing form and honest content — is the ideal of wisdom literature: the words should be both artistically satisfying and truthfully accurate. The form serves the content; the content requires honest investigation; both together are the cheifetz (delight) the Teacher sought.
11: divrei chakhamim kadarbenot: 'The words of the wise are like goads.' Darbenot (goads) — the pointed stick that moves animals; wisdom-words that prod the mind toward action. Uchemasmerot netu'im ba'alei asufot: 'and like firmly driven nails are the collected sayings.' Ba'alei asufot (masters of collections, members of collections) — the assembled body of wisdom sayings. Nittenu mere'eh echad: 'given by one Shepherd.' Ro'eh echad (one Shepherd) — the single source behind the multiple wisdom traditions. Qohelet here draws together all the wisdom teaching under the image of the one Shepherd — the divine source from whom all wisdom ultimately derives, regardless of its human compilers.
12: veyoter meheimah beni hizzaher: 'Beyond these, my son, be warned.' The beni (my son) address echoes the father-son frame of Proverbs 1-9 — wisdom tradition addressing the next generation. Asot sefarim harbeh ein qetz: 'making many books has no end.' The epistemological limit of the written corpus: books multiply without limit. Velagg limmud harbe yegi'at basar: 'and much study is weariness of the flesh.' The study that goes beyond the collected wisdom into endless book-making is identified as bodily exhaustion rather than the profitable labor (yitron amal) Qohelet has sought throughout. The warning is not against learning but against the mistaking of book-proliferation for wisdom.
13: sof davar hakol nishma: 'The conclusion of the matter — all has been heard.' Sof davar (the end of the matter, the conclusion) — the logical conclusion drawn from everything Qohelet has observed and said. Et ha'elohim yera ve'et mitzvotav shemor ki zeh kol ha'adam: 'Fear God and keep his commandments — for this is the whole of man.' Kol ha'adam (the whole of man, the entirety of man) — not merely the duty but the definition: this is what the human being finally is, in sum. The yirat elohim (fear of God) that Proverbs 1:7 places at the beginning as the start of wisdom, Qohelet places at the end as its conclusion — the beginning and end of the wisdom tradition converge on the same foundation.
14: ki et kol ma'aseh yavi ha'elohim bamishpat: 'For God will bring every deed into judgment.' Al kol ne'elam: 'concerning every hidden thing' — ne'elam (hidden, concealed) from alam (to be hidden/silent). The judgment is comprehensive: every action, im tov ve'im ra (whether good or whether evil). Qohelet's conclusion does not rest on epistemological humility alone (we don't know what will happen) but on the theological certainty that God's judgment will ultimately adjudicate what human observation cannot reach. The hidden things that escape human reckoning do not escape the divine accounting. This final verse transforms the entire book's observations about the limits of human knowledge into an argument for moral seriousness under divine sovereignty.
